questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of severe right iliac fossa pain that came on while she was at rest. She feels nauseous and has vomited twice. There is no vaginal discharge or fever. Her last menstrual period was two weeks ago. On examination, there is tenderness in the right iliac fossa with guarding. Urinalysis is normal. Serum β-hCG is negative. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Sudden severe pelvic pain with nausea and vomiting, absence of fever, vaginal discharge, and negative β-hCG make ovarian torsion most likely. Ruptured cyst or appendicitis would often have more localised peritonism or fever; ectopic pregnancy is excluded by negative β-hCG.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman has had three episodes over the past month of severe left-sided pelvic pain lasting 15–20 minutes, each resolving spontaneously after she changes position. Between episodes she is pain-free. She is not pregnant. Ultrasound shows a 6 cm left ovarian cyst with normal Doppler flow. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Recurrent, self-limiting severe pelvic pain that resolves with position change is classic for intermittent torsion/detorsion. Normal Doppler flow between episodes does not exclude torsion. Mittelschmerz is mid-cycle and milder; endometriosis causes chronic pain; IBS is relieved by defecation.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old primigravida at 16 weeks gestation presents with a 3-hour history of sudden, constant right-sided lower abdominal pain. She has vomited once. On examination, there is tenderness over the right side of the uterus with mild rebound. She is afebrile. Fetal heart sounds are normal. Ultrasound shows a single live intrauterine pregnancy and an enlarged right ovary measuring 8 cm with absent venous flow. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: In pregnancy, the ovary is vulnerable to torsion due to displacement by the uterus. Sudden severe pain, vomiting, and sonographic findings of an enlarged ovary with absent venous flow confirm torsion. Round ligament pain is dull and positional; ectopic is excluded by IUP; abruption would involve bleeding and uterine hypertonus.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old postmenopausal woman with a known 5 cm left ovarian cyst presents with acute onset of severe left lower quadrant pain radiating to the left flank. She is nauseated but has not vomited. She is afebrile. On examination, there is tenderness and guarding in the left iliac fossa. Urinalysis shows no blood or nitrites. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Postmenopausal women with an ovarian cyst can still have torsion; the acute severe unilateral pain with nausea and absence of fever or urinary symptoms makes torsion most likely. Ovarian malignancy would present more insidiously; diverticulitis usually has fever and left lower quadrant pain but often diarrhoea; ureteric colic would be colicky with haematuria.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman known to have a right ovarian dermoid cyst presents with sudden severe right iliac fossa pain and vomiting. She is tachycardic but normotensive. On examination, there is marked tenderness and involuntary guarding in the right iliac fossa. Serum β-hCG is negative. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Dermoid cysts are heavy and predispose to torsion. The presentation of sudden severe pain with vomiting and signs of peritonism, in a patient with a known dermoid, makes torsion the most likely diagnosis. Rupture of a dermoid could cause chemical peritonitis but is less common; ectopic is excluded; appendicitis would be less acute with fever.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with sudden onset of severe right iliac fossa pain, nausea, and vomiting. On examination, she is tender in the right lower quadrant with voluntary guarding. A transabdominal ultrasound shows a 7 cm right ovarian cyst with an enlarged ovary. What is the most appropriate next investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Transvaginal ultrasound with colour Doppler is the first-line investigation for suspected ovarian torsion. It can demonstrate an enlarged ovary, free fluid, and absence of arterial or venous flow on Doppler, which is highly suggestive.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with intermittent dull lower abdominal pain over the past two weeks, sometimes associated with nausea. She has no vomiting. Examination is unremarkable. A transvaginal ultrasound with colour Doppler is normal. She remains clinically suspicious for intermittent ovarian torsion. What is the most appropriate next investigation?
questions.explanation: In cases of high clinical suspicion with normal ultrasound, diagnostic laparoscopy is the gold standard to confirm or exclude ovarian torsion as it allows direct visualization of the adnexa.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman with a known 8 cm simple ovarian cyst presents with sudden onset left-sided abdominal pain and vomiting. An urgent transvaginal ultrasound reveals a left ovary enlarged with no arterial or venous flow. Which ultrasound finding is most specific for ovarian torsion?
questions.explanation: The whirlpool sign (twisted vascular pedicle) is pathognomonic for ovarian torsion. While absent Doppler flow is suggestive, it can also be seen in other conditions such as haemorrhage or infarction.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman at 16 weeks gestation presents with acute right lower quadrant pain and vomiting. She has a previous history of ovarian cysts. An ultrasound shows a 6 cm right ovary with some internal flow but reduced compared to the left. What is the most appropriate next investigation?
questions.explanation: MRI pelvis without gadolinium is safe in pregnancy and can provide additional information when ultrasound is equivocal. It has high sensitivity for torsion and avoids radiation.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old woman presents with sudden severe left iliac fossa pain. A transvaginal ultrasound shows a left ovary enlarged to 8 cm with absent venous flow on Doppler but preserved arterial flow. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: In early or partial torsion, the low-pressure venous system is occluded first while high-pressure arterial flow persists. This is a classic sign of incomplete torsion; complete torsion would show absent arterial flow later.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman presents with acute onset right iliac fossa pain. She is afebrile, and ultrasound confirms a 7 cm right ovarian cyst with absent arterial flow, consistent with ovarian torsion. The ovary appears viable with some venous flow. What is the most appropriate first-line management?
questions.explanation: In a young patient with a viable ovary, detorsion is recommended to preserve ovarian function; oophorectomy is reserved for necrotic tissue.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with known 10 cm left ovarian cyst presents with severe lower abdominal pain, vomiting, and fever. She has rebound tenderness. Ultrasound shows no flow in the left ovary, and blood tests show raised white cell count and CRP. What is the most appropriate management?
questions.explanation: Signs of peritonitis and infection suggest ovarian necrosis; oophorectomy is indicated to remove the septic source and prevent further deterioration.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old primigravida at 10 weeks gestation presents with acute left iliac fossa pain. Ultrasound shows a 6 cm homogeneous ovarian cyst and a left ovary with reduced but present arterial flow, consistent with torsion. The pregnancy is viable. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Medical management with analgesics and observation
questions.explanation: Detorsion is the first-line conservative surgery in pregnancy to preserve the ovary and avoid unnecessary risk to the pregnancy; oophorectomy is reserved for non-viable tissue.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with right iliac fossa pain. Ultrasound shows a 12 cm complex cystic and solid ovarian mass with no flow. The contralateral ovary appears normal. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Total abdominal hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy
questions.explanation: In postmenopausal women, risk of malignancy is higher; a complex mass with torsion warrants unilateral oophorectomy rather than detorsion, and hysterectomy is not indicated in the absence of uterine pathology.

questions.stem: A 20-year-old woman presents with a third episode of right ovarian torsion. She previously underwent successful detorsion twice. Ultrasound shows a 5 cm simple cyst and the ovary appears viable. What is the most appropriate definitive management to prevent recurrence?
options: Oral contraceptive pill for ovarian suppression
questions.explanation: Recurrent torsion despite prior detorsion is best managed by oophoropexy (surgical fixation) to stabilise the ovary and prevent future torsion; oophorectomy is too aggressive in a young patient.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with acute onset of sharp right iliac fossa pain, nausea, and two episodes of vomiting. She is afebrile, pulse 88 bpm, BP 118/74. Urine pregnancy test is negative. Abdominal examination reveals tenderness in the right lower quadrant with no guarding. Transabdominal ultrasound shows an enlarged right ovary measuring 6 cm with absent arterial flow on Doppler. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Repeat ultrasound in 6 hours to reassess Doppler flow
Discharge with oral analgesia and outpatient follow-up
Start oral antibiotics for suspected pelvic inflammatory disease
Urgent referral to the gynaecology on-call team for diagnostic laparoscopy
Request CT abdomen with intravenous contrast
questions.explanation: Absent arterial flow on Doppler in a symptomatic woman strongly suggests ovarian torsion, which is a surgical emergency. Urgent gynaecology referral for diagnostic laparoscopy allows detorsion and ovarian preservation. Discharge or repeat imaging risks delay and potential loss of the ovary. Antibiotics are not indicated without evidence of infection, and CT is not the first-line investigation for suspected torsion when ultrasound is diagnostic.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with a known 5 cm simple ovarian cyst presents with a two-day history of intermittent right-sided pelvic pain. Observations are normal: pulse 76, BP 124/78, temperature 36.8°C. Pelvic ultrasound shows a 5 cm simple cyst with reduced but present arterial flow on Doppler. Urine pregnancy test is negative. She is otherwise well. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Admit for conservative management with analgesia and serial Doppler ultrasounds
Discharge with oral NSAIDs and a follow-up ultrasound in 2 weeks
questions.explanation: Reduced but present Doppler flow indicates partial torsion or impending torsion, which can progress. The patient is stable, so admission for observation with serial scans allows monitoring of flow and prompt intervention if symptoms worsen or flow ceases. Discharging her risks delayed recognition of complete torsion. Urgent laparoscopy may be premature if symptoms are intermittent and flow is present; conservative management is appropriate first step. Antibiotics and CT are not indicated.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with severe acute left lower quadrant pain of 2 hours duration, with nausea and vomiting. On examination, she appears unwell, pulse 110 bpm, BP 90/60 mmHg, temperature 37.9°C. There is guarding and rigidity in the left lower quadrant. Urine pregnancy test is negative. Transabdominal ultrasound in the ED shows an enlarged left ovary with absent Doppler flow and free fluid. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Start intravenous broad-spectrum antibiotics and arrange urgent gynaecology review
Administer 1 litre of intravenous crystalloid rapidly and request urgent gynaecology review
questions.explanation: This patient has signs of hypovolaemic shock (tachycardia, hypotension) likely due to ovarian torsion with haemorrhage or infarction. The priority is fluid resuscitation. Rapid IV crystalloid administration stabilises the patient before definitive surgical management. Urgent gynaecology review is needed for surgical detorsion. Antibiotics are secondary. Immediate laparotomy without resuscitation is hazardous; CT would delay necessary resuscitation.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman underwent elective laparoscopic cystectomy for an 8 cm ovarian cyst. At surgery, the ovary was found to be twisted but appeared viable after detorsion. On postoperative day 1, she develops worsening lower abdominal pain and a temperature of 38.5°C. Pulse 98 bpm, BP 110/70. Wound appears clean. Urine output is normal. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Obtain blood cultures and start intravenous gentamicin
Start intravenous co-amoxiclav and arrange urgent CT abdomen with contrast
Arrange urgent pelvic ultrasound with Doppler
Discharge with oral antibiotics and outpatient follow-up
Repeat diagnostic laparoscopy for suspected recurrent torsion
questions.explanation: Postoperative fever and pain after ovarian detorsion raise suspicion for infection (e.g., abscess or infected ovarian tissue). The most appropriate next step is to start broad-spectrum antibiotics (co-amoxiclav is common) and arrange CT to look for an abscess or necrosis. Ultrasound is less sensitive for deep pelvic collections post-surgery. Repeat laparoscopy would be premature without imaging. Discharge is unsafe.

questions.stem: A 16-year-old girl presents with a one-hour history of acute right iliac fossa pain, nausea, and vomiting. She is afebrile, pulse 84, BP 115/75. Urine pregnancy test is negative. Abdominal ultrasound shows a 7 cm complex ovarian cyst with internal echoes and absent Doppler flow. She is otherwise well. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Repeat ultrasound in 12 hours to confirm absent flow
Start intravenous co-amoxiclav for suspected tubo-ovarian abscess
Request CT abdomen with contrast to characterise the cyst
Discharge with oral analgesia and arrange outpatient follow-up for cystectomy
Urgent referral to the gynaecology team for diagnostic laparoscopy
questions.explanation: In a young woman with acute pain and absent Doppler flow, ovarian torsion is highly likely. Urgent gynaecology referral for diagnostic laparoscopy is essential to detorse the ovary and potentially perform oophoropexy. Delaying with repeat imaging or CT risks ovarian necrosis and loss of fertility. Antibiotics are not indicated unless infection is confirmed.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman presents with sudden onset left iliac fossa pain and nausea. She is not pregnant. On examination, she is afebrile, with mild tenderness in the left lower quadrant. Which clinical finding would most increase the suspicion for ovarian torsion?
questions.explanation: Cervical motion tenderness (adnexal tenderness on moving the cervix) is a classic sign of adnexal irritation, commonly seen in ovarian torsion. Rebound tenderness suggests peritonitis (later stage). A palpable mass is non-specific; fever and tachycardia are absent in early torsion.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman with a known 6 cm ovarian cyst presents with acute right iliac fossa pain and vomiting. She has mild tenderness. A transvaginal ultrasound is performed. Which finding is most diagnostic of ovarian torsion?
options: Free fluid in pouch of Douglas
Absence of arterial or venous flow on Doppler
questions.explanation: Absence of ovarian blood flow on Doppler ultrasound is the most specific finding for torsion, indicating compromised vascular supply. Enlargement and free fluid are suggestive but not diagnostic; thickened cyst wall can occur in other conditions; ovarian ring sign is seen in ectopic pregnancy.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old primigravida at 10 weeks gestation presents with left-sided pelvic pain and nausea. She is haemodynamically stable. What clinical feature would raise the strongest suspicion for ovarian torsion?
questions.explanation: Cervical motion tenderness indicates adnexal irritation, typical of torsion. In pregnancy, round ligament pain does not cause this sign; vaginal bleeding suggests miscarriage; fever suggests infection; suprapubic tenderness and dysuria point to urinary causes.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman with no known ovarian issues presents with acute right iliac fossa pain and vomiting. She appears unwell, with BP 90/60 mmHg, HR 110/min, temperature 38.5°C. On examination, she has rebound tenderness and guarding. Which life-threatening complication should be suspected?
questions.explanation: The combination of acute pain, vomiting, fever, hypotension, and peritonitis (rebound, guarding) in a perimenopausal woman suggests ovarian torsion leading to infarction and sepsis. Ectopic pregnancy is less likely without pregnancy or vaginal bleeding; ruptured cyst may cause peritonitis but less fever; PID often bilateral; appendicitis usually presents with migration of pain.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old woman presents with sudden severe left iliac fossa pain and nausea. She is not sexually active. On exam, she is afebrile with mild tenderness. Urine pregnancy test is negative. Which symptom is most characteristic of ovarian torsion?
questions.explanation: Vomiting is a classic accompanying symptom in ovarian torsion due to peritoneal irritation. Diarrhoea is more typical of gastroenteritis; dysuria suggests UTI; back pain may be renal; headache is unrelated.
